title: Reliability comparison of tantalum and niobium solid electrolytic capacitors
abstract:
QUALITY AND RELIABILITY ENGINEERING INTERNATIONAL
Qual. Reliab. Engng. Int. 14: 79–82 (1998)
RELIABILITY COMPARISON OF TANTALUM AND NIOBIUM SOLID
ELECTROLYTIC CAPACITORS
Y. POZDEEV
Vishay-Sprague, VIEC, Industrial Park, Dimona 86000, Israel
SUMMARY
This paper deals with a comparative investigation of tantalum and niobium solid electrolytic capacitors. Nb is an
attractive replacement for Ta in solid electrolytic capacitors because it is lighter and cheaper than Ta. Although
these two metals have much in common in their crystalline structure and physical and chemical properties, the
electrical properties of Ta and Nb capacitors are different. Particularly, most Nb capacitors are characterized by an
increase in direct current leakage during life testing. This causes parametric failure of Nb capacitors. On the other
hand, the direct current leakage of Ta capacitors does not change signiﬁcantly for a long time, but then increases
sharply for some samples. Hence occasional catastrophic failures are typical for Ta capacitors. Nevertheless, the
properties of high-CV Ta capacitors with low and high rated voltage approach the properties of Nb capacitors. The
physical nature of these phenomena is discussed. 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
KEY WORDS:
capacitors; tantalum; niobium; reliability
1.
INTRODUCTION
Solid tantalum capacitors with porous anodes sintered in
vacuum from ﬁne Ta powder have the greatest capaci-
tance per unit volume among similar capacitors [1]. They
also possess high reliability and good AC characteristics.
However, the cost and weight per unit capacitance for
tantalum capacitors are more than for other capacitor
types, e.g. aluminium capacitors.
Tantalum and niobium are neighbours in group 5a of
the periodic table. They have the same type of crystalline
lattice as well as an almost identical atomic radius and
lattice parameter (Table 1). However, the atomic mass
and density of Nb are about half those of Ta. This is the
reason for the reduced weight of Nb capacitors compared
with Ta capacitors. Nb capacitors are also less expensive
to manufacture.
The capacitance C per unit anode surface can be ap-
proximately expressed as C = ε/d, where ε is the low-
frequency dielectric permeability and d is the thickness
of the anodic oxide ﬁlm formed on the anode surface [2].
The d value is directly proportional to the formation
voltage Vf : d = aVf, where a is the formation constant.
From Table 1 one can see that the ratio ε/a for Ta2O5
ﬁlm is almost identical to that for Nb2O5 ﬁlm. The Vf
value is chosen in the same manner for both Ta and
Nb capacitors, depending on the rated voltage. That is
why the C value and thereby the volumetric efﬁciency
are about the same for both Ta and Nb capacitors using
similar powder grades.
2.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Utilization of Nb as the anode metal for solid electrolytic
capacitors is limited by direct current leakage (DCL) non-
*Correspondence to: Y. Pozdeev, Vishay-Sprague, VIEC, Industrial
Park, Dimona 86000, Israel.

stability during high-temperature processing and electri-
cal loading. For example, Figure 1 presents typical life
test results for Ta and Nb capacitors manufactured by
the same ‘tantalum’ process from similar powder grades.
In this ﬁgure, DCL values after life testing are plotted
against initial DCL values before life testing. For Ta
capacitors (Figure 1(a)) most of the experimental points
lie along the diagonal, indicating high DCL stability. For
Nb capacitors (Figure 1(b)) the experimental points are
shifted upwards with respect to the diagonal, indicating
DCL increase during life testing. This leads to parametric
failure of Nb capacitors.
There are two major degradation processes responsi-
ble for DCL increase in both Ta and Nb capacitors [3,4].
The ﬁrst is the process of oxygen extraction from the
Me2O5 ﬁlm by the Me anode (Me ≡Ta, Nb). This pro-
cess is originated by thermodynamic non-equilibrium of
the Me/Me2O5 pair. The conductivity of the reduced
Me2O5−x ﬁlm becomes greater with increasing oxygen
deﬁcit x, because oxygen vacancies act as donor centres,
injecting current carriers to the dielectric. The second
degradation process is amorphous Me2O5 ﬁlm crystal-
lization originated by thermodynamic non-equilibrium of
the amorphous state. Crystallization leads to amorphous
CCC 0748–8017/98/020079–04$17.50
Received 12 November 1997
1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Revised 10 January 1998
 80
Y. POZDEEV
Figure 1. Typical 1000 h life test results for (a) Ta and (b) Nb capacitors manufactured by same ‘tantalum’ process
matrix disruption and thereby to DCL increase.
Despite the fact that the nature of the degradation
processes is identical for both Ta and Nb capacitors,
the degradation rates in these capacitors are quite differ-
ent [3]. This causes different consequences of the degra-
dation processes for Ta and Nb capacitors, especially
different DCL behaviour during life testing as shown in
Figure 1.
2.1
Oxygen transport
One of the main reasons for different degradation rates
in these capacitors is the higher capability of oxygen
absorption by Nb in comparison with Ta [5]. As a result,
the process of oxygen extraction from the Nb2O5 ﬁlm by
the Nb anode in Nb capacitors is more intensive than the
same process in Ta capacitors. Owing to the low reduc-
tion rate, the oxygen deﬁcit created in the Ta2O5 ﬁlm
can be completely compensated by the oxygen stream
from an MnO2 cathode, which possesses solid electrolyte
characteristics. Hence dynamic equilibrium of the oxygen
content in the Ta2O5 ﬁlm is provided, allowing stable
DCL values during life testing of Ta capacitors.
In Nb capacitors the rate of oxygen extraction from the
Nb2O5 ﬁlm by the Nb anode is actually higher than the
feasible oxygen supply to this ﬁlm by the MnO2 cathode.
In this case the oxygen deﬁcit x in the reduced Nb2O5−x
ﬁlm increases over time, leading to DCL increase. This is
not a monotonic process. Low manganese oxide phases
(Mn2O3, Mn3O4, MnO) appear in the vicinity of the
MnO2–Nb2O5 interface, suppressing further oxygen dif-
fusion from the MnO2 cathode to the reduced Nb2O5−x
ﬁlm [6]. This leads to accelerated x increase and thereby
to accelerated DCL increase and capacitor failure.
The oxygen transport in a Ta (Nb) capacitor is shown
schematically in Figure 2, where stream 1 relates to the
redox reaction at the Me–Me2O5 interface, stream 2 re-
lates to the diffusion of oxygen ions through the Me2O5
ﬁlm and stream 3 relates to the redox reaction at the
MnO2/Me2O5 interface. From Figure 2 one can realize
the nature of the galvanic cell property (current gen-
eration) inherent in Ta and Nb capacitors at elevated
temperatures. According to deﬁnitions for this property,
two redox reactions separated by an ion conductor are
required [7]. This phenomenon allows the experimental
Figure 2. Scheme of oxygen transport in Ta (Nb) capacitor temperature
determination of the oxygen ion current Ii through the
Me2O5 ﬁlm. The Ii value is equal to the short-circuit
current Isc in the external circuit (Figure 2).
Figure 3 shows the temperature dependence of the
short-circuit current for Ta and Nb capacitors with the
same CV and Me2O5 ﬁlm thickness. One can see that
the Ta capacitor has a higher activation energy of oxygen
transport as well as lower Isc values at equal temperatures
in comparison with Nb capacitor. This is experimental
evidence that oxygen transport through the anodic oxide
ﬁlm in the Nb capacitor is more intensive than in the Ta
capacitor. As a result, the balance of oxygen content in
the anodic oxide ﬁlm exists for a longer time in the Ta
capacitor. However, the oxygen deﬁcit x in the Nb2O5−x
ﬁlm increases over time in the Nb capacitor, leading to
DCL increase and capacitor failure.
2.2
Crystallization
Crystallization of the amorphous anodic oxide ﬁlm in
Nb capacitors is also more intensive than in Ta capaci-
tors. This is because of the higher oxygen absorption of
Nb as well as its lower melting temperature and higher
formation constant (Table 1). The absorption of oxygen
from the residual atmosphere of the vacuum furnace leads
to Nb anode supersaturation with oxygen and thereby to
local precipitation of oxide phase particles. These parti-
cles are incorporated in the Nb2O5 ﬁlm during its forma-
tion and act as crystallization centres for the amorphous
matrix.
1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Qual. Reliab. Engng. Int. 14: 79–82 (1998)

stability during high-temperature processing and electri-
cal loading. For example, Figure 1 presents typical life
test results for Ta and Nb capacitors manufactured by
the same ‘tantalum’ process from similar powder grades.

Despite the fact that the nature of the degradation

ent [3]. This causes different consequences of the degra-
dation processes for Ta and Nb capacitors, especially

One of the main reasons for different degradation rates
in these capacitors is the higher capability of oxygen
absorption by Nb in comparison with Ta [5]. As a result,
the process of oxygen extraction from the Nb2O5 ﬁlm by
the Nb anode in Nb capacitors is more intensive than the
same process in Ta capacitors. Owing to the low reduc-
tion rate, the oxygen deﬁcit created in the Ta2O5 ﬁlm
can be completely compensated by the oxygen stream
from an MnO2 cathode, which possesses solid electrolyte
characteristics. Hence dynamic equilibrium of the oxygen
content in the Ta2O5 ﬁlm is provided, allowing stable
DCL values during life testing of Ta capacitors.
In Nb capacitors the rate of oxygen extraction from the
Nb2O5 ﬁlm by the Nb anode is actually higher than the
feasible oxygen supply to this ﬁlm by the MnO2 cathode.
In this case the oxygen deﬁcit x in the reduced Nb2O5−x
ﬁlm increases over time, leading to DCL increase. This is
not a monotonic process. Low manganese oxide phases
(Mn2O3, Mn3O4, MnO) appear in the vicinity of the
MnO2–Nb2O5 interface, suppressing further oxygen dif-
fusion from the MnO2 cathode to the reduced Nb2O5−x
ﬁlm [6]. This leads to accelerated x increase and thereby
to accelerated DCL increase and capacitor failure.
The oxygen transport in a Ta (Nb) capacitor is shown
schematically in Figure 2, where stream 1 relates to the
redox reaction at the Me–Me2O5 interface, stream 2 re-
lates to the diffusion of oxygen ions through the Me2O5
ﬁlm and stream 3 relates to the redox reaction at the
MnO2/Me2O5 interface. From Figure 2 one can realize
the nature of the galvanic cell property (current gen-
eration) inherent in Ta and Nb capacitors at elevated
temperatures. According to deﬁnitions for this property,
two redox reactions separated by an ion conductor are
required [7]. This phenomenon allows the experimental
Figure 2. Scheme of oxygen transport in Ta (Nb) capacitor temperature
determination of the oxygen ion current Ii through the
Me2O5 ﬁlm. The Ii value is equal to the short-circuit
current Isc in the external circuit (Figure 2).
Figure 3 shows the temperature dependence of the
short-circuit current for Ta and Nb capacitors with the
same CV and Me2O5 ﬁlm thickness. One can see that
the Ta capacitor has a higher activation energy of oxygen
transport as well as lower Isc values at equal temperatures
in comparison with Nb capacitor. This is experimental
evidence that oxygen transport through the anodic oxide
ﬁlm in the Nb capacitor is more intensive than in the Ta
capacitor. As a result, the balance of oxygen content in
the anodic oxide ﬁlm exists for a longer time in the Ta
capacitor. However, the oxygen deﬁcit x in the Nb2O5−x
ﬁlm increases over time in the Nb capacitor, leading to
DCL increase and capacitor failure.
Crystallization of the amorphous anodic oxide ﬁlm in
Nb capacitors is also more intensive than in Ta capaci-
tors. This is because of the higher oxygen absorption of
Nb as well as its lower melting temperature and higher
formation constant (Table 1). The absorption of oxygen
from the residual atmosphere of the vacuum furnace leads
to Nb anode supersaturation with oxygen and thereby to
local precipitation of oxide phase particles. These parti-
cles are incorporated in the Nb2O5 ﬁlm during its forma-
tion and act as crystallization centres for the amorphous
RELIABILITY OF SOLID CAPACITORS
Figure 3. Isc dependence on inverse temperature
The lower melting temperature of Nb causes excessive
Nb anode shrinkage in comparison with Ta at equal sin-
tering temperatures. That is why, for the same CV, Nb
anodes should be sintered at temperatures lower than for
Ta. For a given temperature range the rate of oxygen ab-
sorption becomes greater with decreasing sintering tem-
perature [4]. This is the additional reason for Nb anodes
becoming supersaturated with oxygen during sintering.
The difference in formation constant between Ta and
Nb means that for the same formation voltage the thick-
ness of the Nb2O5 ﬁlm is about 1.5 times that of the
Ta2O5 ﬁlm. It is well known that the susceptibility to
crystallization of the amorphous ﬁlms becomes greater
with increasing thickness [2]. That is why the upper limit
for rated voltage in Nb capacitors is less than in Ta
Despite the lower rate of the degradation processes in
Ta capacitors in comparison with Nb capacitors, occa-
sional failures do occur for Ta capacitors also, in the form
of sudden, sharp DCL increase (catastrophic type) [8].
For most events these failures are originated by local pen-
etration of either silver or graphite (top cathode coatings)
through pores and cracks in the cathode to the Ta2O5 ﬁlm
(Figure 4). In the areas of such penetration the inherent
capacitor healing effect is blocked, leading to the Ta2O5
ﬁlm deterioration and further electrical breakdown. As
the integral surface of these areas is negligible compared
with the complete anode surface, the DCL practically
does not change before breakdown. The moment in time
when such failure will occur depends on the existence of
latent defects in the Ta2O5 ﬁlm below the silver (graphite)
Experimental evidence of this failure mechanism
was obtained by use of a scanning electron microscope
(SEM). The small areas of either silver or graphite
penetration to the anode surface were detected by the
SEM on cross-sections of failed capacitors. Typically
these areas are located at sites of MnO2 thinning and in
pores or cracks caused by improper cathode deposition.
After the cathode was stripped a burnt spot was revealed
on the anode surface around the breakdown channel.
Figure 4. Scheme of failure mechanism in Ta capacitor caused by silver
The above discussion deals with differences between
Ta and Nb capacitors. However, two cases where Ta ca-
pacitors demonstrate properties typical of Nb capacitors
were detected. The ﬁrst case relates to Ta capacitors, man-
ufactured from high-CV powder. As the powder particles’
radius decreases with increasing CV, the anode’s suscep-
tibility to oxygen absorption increases and the sintering
temperature decreases, similar to regular Nb anodes [4].
Utilization of high-CV anodes is especially difﬁcult for
either high- or low-rated-voltage capacitors. The rela-
tively thick Ta2O5 ﬁlm formed on the anode in high-
rated-voltage capacitors has enhanced susceptibility to
crystallization. The thin Ta2O5 ﬁlm in low-rated-voltage
capacitors is transparent to oxygen diffusion, because the
diffusion stream becomes greater with decreasing ﬁlm
thickness. All these phenomena cause DCL increase in
Ta capacitors during high temperature processing and
electrical loading, similar to Nb capacitors.
The second case relates to Ta capacitors with welded-
type anodes (Ta lead wire welded to sintered Ta anode).
An Nb-enriched thin shell around the welding zone was
detected in such anodes. Figure 5 shows a welded-type Ta
anode after its formation at Vf = 50 V. One can see a dark
hemispherical shell around the welding zone. The high
Nb content of this shell was measured by means of X-
ray microanalysis. The anodic oxide ﬁlm’s colour inside
the shell is dark red and outside the shell is light yellow.
According to the colour scale, the ratio between the oxide
ﬁlm thickness inside the shell and that outside the shell
is about 1.5:1. This ratio is the same for all Vf values
and conforms to the ratio between Nb and Ta formation
A possible interpretation of this phenomenon could be
the following. Niobium is a typical substitution impurity
in tantalum. During the welding process the high-current-
density impulse can push Nb from the welding zone to
the thin shell on its periphery. After lead welding, the
Ta anode is subjected to high-temperature annealing in
vacuum. This second sintering redistributes Nb and other
impurities from the shell to the Ta anode volume (the
Nb peak was not detected around the welding zone after
the second sintering). Hence the second sintering has
a negative consequence for Ta capacitors. The oxygen
content of the Ta anode increases after the second sinter-
Figure 5. (a) Top and (b) fracture of welded-type Ta anode after formation of anodic oxide ﬁlm (Vf = 50 V)
ing, and this effect is especially pronounced for high-CV
Based on this study, the following two conditions are
required for the manufacture of reliable Nb capacitors:
(a) protection of the Nb anode from supersaturation with
(b) suppression of oxygen transport through the Nb2O5
If these conditions are satisﬁed (‘niobium’ process),
Nb capacitors with stable DCL and high reliability can
be manufactured. The preferable speciﬁcations for Nb
capacitors include not very high CV and moderate rated
voltage. In addition, the ‘niobium’ process is useful for
the manufacture of high-CV Ta capacitors because of the
similarity of the physical properties of conventional Nb
The author thanks Mr P. Maden from Vishay-Sprague,
Dr M. Belman from Vishay-Dale and Drs A. Palevsky,
A. Gladkikh and M. Karpovsky from Tel-Aviv University
for helpful discussion and collaboration in experiments

3. B. T. Boiko, Y. Pozdeev, et al., Thin Solid Films, 229, 207 (1993).
4. Y. Pozdeev, Proc. 10th Eur. Passive Components Symp., Nice, 1996.
5. E. Fromm and E. Gebhard, Gase und Kohlenstoff in Metallen,
6. B. T. Boiko, Y. Pozdeev et al., Thin Solid Films, 130 341 (1985).
7. F. A. Kroger, The Chemistry of Imperfect Crystals, North-Holland,
8. P. -O. Fagerholt, Proc. 17th US Capacitor and Resistor Symp.,
Yuri Pozdeev has about 25 years experience as a process en-
gineer and as a researcher in electronic industry of the former
Soviet Union and in Vishay Israel Electronic Company (since
1995). The major subject of his work is physical principles
of electronic components (particularly tantalum and niobium
capacitors). This is considered as the basis for development of
advanced products and technology. He has about 35 publica-
